category_title: Emotionally disturbed first-grader with trauma-induced behavioral challenges
minimal_context: Jamal is sitting at his desk in a class of 25 students and is asked to write his numbers on a piece of paper.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Jamal, please sit at your desk and write your numbers.

STUDENT: (Loudly, seeking attention from the rest of the class) I don't want to write numbers!

TUTOR: You need to practice writing them. Go ahead and start on your paper.

STUDENT: (Tears his paper)

TUTOR: Let's get a new piece of paper and try again.

STUDENT: (Falls into a heap and cries) My daddy was killed! (Kicks at his desk)

options.A: Adjust seating arrangements by moving the student closer to the teacher's desk and next to a well-behaved peer in the class of 25 students.
options.B: Combine photo elicitation with life-story splicing of narrative inquiry by sharing photographs of families and telling stories about these photographs with the student.
options.C: Use large fonts with supporting pictures, reduce the amount of numbers to write, provide access to number banks, and incorporate speech-generating devices.
options.D: Provide specific times for purposeful attention through noncontingent reinforcement (NCR) by scheduling frequent, free access to peer attention throughout the school day.
